title: Evidence for temporal virulence change in pearl millet downy mildew pathogen populations in India
abstract:
Downy mildew (DM) caused by Sclerospora graminicola
is prevalent in most pearl millet (Pennisetum glaucum)
growing parts of the semi-arid tropics. The pathogen is an
obligate oomycetes and reproduces both by sexual and
asexual processes. Heterothallism and sexual cross
compatibility among the isolates of S. graminicola
(Michelmore et al. 1982, Thakur et al. 2004, Pushpavathi
et al. 2006a) lead to genetic and pathogenic variability in
the pathogen population. Sporangia, the asexual spores,
produced on the infected leaves help in secondary spread
of the disease. In India, the disease is quite severe on
single-cross F1 hybrids and causes substantial yield losses
(Singh 1995, Thakur et al. 2003).
